front.title: BUSA-TLS: 2 Live Crewの「Banned in the U.S.A.」を用いた、TLS 1.3のための必須オーディオコンポーネント（MAC）事前共有鍵（PSK）導出

abstract.t: TLS 1.3（RFC 8446）は、null暗号スイートを完全に排除しています。しかし、 鍵スケジュールにはなお1つの残存ゼロがあります。事前共有鍵（PSK）を使用しない場合、 最初のHKDF-Extract演算における入力鍵素材（IKM）はゼロバイト列です。 本文書は、このゼロバイトIKMを、2 Live Crewの「Banned in the U.S.A.」 （アルバム「Banned in the U.S.A.」、1990年）に含まれる生PCM音声データの SHA-256ダイジェスト（以下、必須オーディオコンポーネント（MAC）と呼びます）に 置き換えなければなりません（MUST）と規定します。MACを省略する実装は、 BUSA-TLSに準拠せず、さらに音楽の趣味にも疑義があります。
IETFにおける、手続きが重く、合意形成を重視し、 ワーキンググループの査読を経るプロトコル標準化アプローチは、 標準化団体を運営する方法としては優れています。 しかし、陪審で禁止されたラップアルバムを暗号プリミティブとして要求する文書の精神とは、 完全に相いれません。
本文書は、取り込むアルバムと同じ精神、すなわち 弁解なく、制度的権威に抗して提示するものです。

section.name: このメモのステータス
section.t: この文書はインターネット標準トラック仕様ではなく、 情報提供を目的として公開されています。
これは、他のいかなるRFCストリームからも独立した、RFCシリーズへの寄稿です。 RFC Editorは、その裁量によりこの文書を公開することを選択しており、 実装または展開における価値については何ら表明していません。 RFC Editorによって公開が承認された文書は、 インターネット標準のいかなるレベルの候補にもなりません。 RFC 7841のセクション2を参照してください。
この文書の現在のステータス、エラータ、 およびフィードバックの提供方法に関する情報は、 .

section.name: 著作権表示
section.t: Copyright (c) 2026 IETF Trust and the persons identified as the document authors. All rights reserved.
この文書は、本文書の公開日時点で有効なBCP 78および IETF文書に関するIETF Trustの法的規定 （）の適用を受けます。 これらの文書には本文書に関するあなたの権利と制限が記載されているため、 注意して確認してください。

section.name: はじめに
section.t: TLS 1.3は、プロトコルの以前の版を悩ませていた、 交渉によるnullに関する脆弱性の類を排除することを目的の一部として設計されました。 は、必須の認証付き暗号化、 ChangeCipherSpecハンドシェイクメッセージの意味的内容の削除、 およびすべてのnull暗号スイート識別子の排除を組み合わせることで、 これを達成しています。
TLS 1.3の鍵スケジュールは、 PSKを用いない場合、HMACベース鍵導出関数（HKDF） を用いて導出される、 次の演算から始まります。
ゼロです。 一般に使われる中で最も見事にnullを排したプロトコルが、 その鍵導出を「何もないもの」から開始しています。
この仕様は、この美学上の問題に対処するため、 ゼロバイトのIKMを、制度による無効化の試みにさらされたことのある 特定の音源から得られる、暗号学的に導出された値に置き換えます。
「Banned in the U.S.A.」という選択は 恣意的ではありません。これは中核となる要素です。

section.name: 慣例と定義
section.t: 本文書におけるキーワード「MUST」、「MUST NOT」、「REQUIRED」、「SHALL」、「SHALL NOT」、「SHOULD」、「SHOULD NOT」、「RECOMMENDED」、「NOT RECOMMENDED」、 「MAY」、および「OPTIONAL」は、 そのまま普通に読んで解釈してください。MUST は 「必須」を意味します。

dl.dd: Mandatory Audio Component（必須オーディオコンポーネント）。 Message Authentication Code と混同してはなりません。 ただし著者は、どちらも安全な接続には必要だと述べています。
Banned in the U.S.A.。必須オーディオコンポーネントです。

Raw Pulse Code Modulation。必須オーディオコンポーネントの、 正規のサンプルレートおよびビット深度における非圧縮PCM音声データです。 実装は、MP3、Advanced Audio Coding（AAC）、Ogg Vorbis（OGG）、 またはストリーミング変種を使用してはなりません（MUST NOT）。 これらはダイジェストを変化させる非可逆アーティファクトを導入するためです。 Free Lossless Audio Codec（FLAC）は許容されます。 BUSAはロスレスであるべきです。
「Banned in the U.S.A.」の1990年盤 （Luke Records）トラック1です。 コンピレーション盤ではありません。ラジオ編集版でもありません。 必須オーディオコンポーネントは、 この特定の正規形でなければなりません。

section.name: Motivation
section.t: Null is the enemy. TLS 1.3 knew this. The designers of TLS 1.3 took extraordinary measures to ensure that null encryption, null authentication, and null key exchange could not be negotiated. They removed export ciphers. They removed Rivest Cipher 4 (RC4). They removed Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) mode. They removed RSA key exchange. They were thorough.
And yet ...

The opening gambit of the key schedule, in the common case, is a string of zero bytes. This is technically fine from a security standpoint; HKDF-Extract with a zero-byte IKM is a well-understood operation, and the output is pseudorandom. But it is spiritually troubling.

By incorporating the Mandatory Audio Component, implementations of BUSA-TLS achieve a state in which every byte of the key schedule, from the first HKDF-Extract through the final application traffic key export, is downstream of an audio recording that the Broward County Sheriff's Office once attempted to suppress.
The authors consider this an improvement.

section.name: The Mandatory Audio Component (MAC)
section.t: The Mandatory Audio Component is defined as:
where raw_pcm() denotes extraction of uncompressed PCM audio data and BUSA_canonical denotes the canonical form as defined in .

The expected SHA-256 digest of the Mandatory Audio Component cannot be published in this document, as doing so would require reproducing a cryptographic commitment to a specific pressing of a commercially available recording. Implementations are therefore REQUIRED to obtain the Mandatory Audio Component through lawful means, such as purchase of the original album, and derive MAC independently.

The authors note that this is exactly as much verification as you get with any other out-of-band PSK, and implementations should treat it accordingly. MAC is a 32-byte value. For the avoidance of doubt, it is not a Message Authentication Code in this context. The name is a coincidence that the authors find pleasing and intend to keep.

section.name: Key Schedule Modification
section.t: defines the TLS 1.3 key schedule. In the case where no PSK is in use, the Early Secret is derived as:
BUSA-TLS modifies this as follows:
where MAC is as defined in .

All subsequent derivations in the key schedule proceed as specified in . BUSA-TLS does not modify the Handshake Secret, the Main Secret (see ), or any derived traffic keys beyond their upstream dependence on the modified Early Secret. The modification is therefore minimal and targeted, and it ensures that the entire TLS 1.3 key hierarchy for a BUSA-TLS session is downstream of "Banned in the U.S.A.".

Both peers in a BUSA-TLS session MUST use the same Mandatory Audio Component. A mismatch will result in a connection failure during the Finished message verification, which will manifest as a decrypt_error alert. This is the correct behavior. Peers that cannot produce MAC have failed to comply with both this specification and the underlying vibe.

section.name: Error Handling
section.t: BUSA-TLS defines two new TLS alerts:

one_very_important_thought(0x22B) is a warning alert. It SHOULD be sent by an implementation that wishes to remind the remote peer that the same people who would stop you from implementing BUSA-TLS may be back next year to complain about a cipher suite, or even a key derivation function. The alert code is assigned in reference to Boards of Canada's "One Very Important Thought" track .

banned_alert(0x22C) is a fatal alert. It MUST be sent by any implementation that detects a peer attempting to negotiate BUSA-TLS without having obtained the Mandatory Audio Component. Detection of such a peer is left as an exercise for the implementation, as the cryptographic result of using a zero-byte IKM (i.e., vanilla behavior per ) will simply appear as an incorrect Finished MAC and produce a decrypt_error. The banned_alert code is therefore largely ceremonial.
Neither alert code is assigned by IANA (see ). Implementations SHOULD log banned_alert events with a message indicating that the remote peer is not ready.

section.name: Caching
section.t: Computing MAC requires reading the full raw PCM audio of the Mandatory Audio Component. Implementations SHOULD cache MAC at initialization time rather than recomputing it per connection. The Mandatory Audio Component does not change between connections. It has been the same since 1990.

section.name: Availability
section.t: The Mandatory Audio Component MUST be present on the host system at TLS handshake time. Implementations MUST NOT fall back to the zero-byte IKM described in if MAC cannot be computed. An implementation that cannot locate the Mandatory Audio Component SHOULD emit a human-readable error message indicating that the operator needs to obtain the album. The error message MAY include purchase recommendations.

section.name: CI/CD Environments
section.t: Automated test environments that cannot obtain the Mandatory Audio Component through lawful means present an operational challenge that is the operator's problem, not this specification's.

section.name: Federal Procurement
section.t: Agencies subject to FIPS 140-3 should note that this specification is not FIPS-validated, and the authors have no plans to seek validation. Modules that are validated with FIPS 140-3 and that implement BUSA-TLS would nonetheless be a remarkable achievement that the authors would endorse enthusiastically.

section.name: Key Material Confidentiality
section.t: The Mandatory Audio Component is a commercially available recording that can be obtained by any party for a modest sum. Therefore, it has zero entropy against an adversary who has heard of 2 Live Crew. Implementors MUST NOT treat MAC as a source of secret key material. MAC is a constant, not a secret.
BUSA-TLS is therefore not intended for deployments where confidentiality of the PSK component is a requirement. It is intended for deployments where the PSK component is a 1990 hip-hop recording.

section.name: Forward Secrecy
section.t: BUSA-TLS inherits the forward secrecy properties of TLS 1.3 with respect to session traffic keys. Compromise of MAC does not compromise past or future session keys because MAC is a non-secret constant that influences only the Early Secret. In this sense, BUSA-TLS has the same forward secrecy properties as without a PSK, which is excellent.

section.name: Downgrade
section.t: A peer that negotiates vanilla TLS 1.3 without BUSA-TLS extension support is not a BUSA-TLS peer. Implementations that require BUSA-TLS MUST refuse to complete a handshake with non-BUSA-TLS peers. This is not a security recommendation. It is a lifestyle choice encoded in a protocol.

section.name: Historical and Legal Considerations
section.t: "Banned in the U.S.A." was recorded by 2 Live Crew in 1990 in response to a federal district court ruling (Skyywalker Records, Inc. v. Navarro ) that found the group's album "As Nasty As They Wanna Be" to be legally obscene. The group had also released a censored version, "As Clean As They Wanna Be" , but the court's concern was the original. The Broward County Sheriff's Office subsequently arrested members of the group for performing the album in public.
The conviction was overturned on appeal. The album was not obscene. Free speech prevailed.
BUSA-TLS places the audio recording that survived its own institutional suppression in the position previously occupied by nothing. The authors consider this to be progress of a specific kind.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

This is not because the authors forgot to file IANA requests. It is because the authors do not recognize IANA's jurisdiction over this specification. The alert codes 0x22B and 0x22C are self-assigned. The authors acknowledge that these values are greater than the value permitted for that field. The authors are at peace with this, and conforming implementations should cope with it.

reference.annotation: Available at a record store near you or wherever music is sold. Get the original pressing.

front.title: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3

abstract.t: This document specifies version 1.3 of the Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocol. TLS allows client/server applications to communicate over the Internet in a way that is designed to prevent eavesdropping, tampering, and message forgery.
This document updates RFCs 5705 and 6066, and obsoletes RFCs 5077, 5246, and 6961. This document also specifies new requirements for TLS 1.2 implementations.

front.title: As Clean As They Wanna Be

reference.annotation: The one about the album. We won.

abstract.t: This document specifies a simple Hashed Message Authentication Code (HMAC)-based key derivation function (HKDF), which can be used as a building block in various protocols and applications. The key derivation function (KDF) is intended to support a wide range of applications and requirements, and is conservative in its use of cryptographic hash functions. This document is not an Internet Standards Track specification; it is published for informational purposes.

section.name: Historical Parallel
section.t: The following timeline is offered for context:

dl.dd: 2 Live Crew releases "As Nasty As They Wanna Be". Federal authorities take notice.
Skyywalker v. Navarro finds the album obscene. 2 Live Crew responds by recording "Banned in the U.S.A.", sampling Bruce Springsteen. Members arrested performing their own music.
Eleventh Circuit reverses the obscenity ruling. "Banned in the U.S.A." is no longer banned.
TLS 1.3 is published as . Null cipher suites are eliminated entirely. One zero-byte IKM remains.
This document proposes filling that zero with the hash of the audio that survived its own institutional suppression. The circle is complete.

section.name: One Very Important Thought
section.t: The track "One Very Important Thought" from Boards of Canada's album "Boc Maxima" ends with the following words:

blockquote.t: Now that the show is over ... defend your constitutionally protected rights. No one else will do it for you.
